title: Tenerife 1977: 583 People Died in Fog on a Runway — The Deadliest Aviation Accident Ever
description: On March 27, 1977, two Boeing 747s collided on a runway at Tenerife Airport. 583 people died. The KLM captain began his takeoff without clearance. The Pan Am crew didn't know where they were on the runway. The controller couldn't see them. A chain of radio miscommunications in fog turned an evacuation diversion into the worst aviation accident in history. #TenerifeDisaster #AviationDisaster #Runway Collision #DisasterDecode

segments.voice: March 27, 1977 holds the terrible distinction of being the deadliest day in the history of commercial aviation. On that Sunday afternoon, on a runway at Los Rodeos Airport in the Canary Islands, two Boeing 747s — one operated by KLM, one by Pan American — collided during a takeoff roll. Five hundred and eighty-three people died. Sixty-one people aboard the Pan Am aircraft survived. None survived from the KLM jet. The accident happened not because of mechanical failure or structural weakness — but because of fog, bad luck, a bomb in a terminal on another island, and a series of radio communications that were each, individually, almost correct.
segments.visual: Tenerife airport fog runway 747 airport Canary Islands 1977

segments.voice: The day began on Gran Canaria — a different Canary Island — where a bomb had exploded at Las Palmas Airport in the terminal building. The airport was closed. All aircraft that had been approaching Las Palmas were diverted to the nearest available airport: Los Rodeos on Tenerife. Los Rodeos was a smaller airport, not designed to handle the volume of large aircraft suddenly parked on its taxiways. Aircraft were so numerous that the taxiway from the terminal to the runway was blocked, requiring aircraft to taxi along the runway itself to reach the runway's starting position.
segments.visual: Gran Canaria bomb Las Palmas diversion airport overcrowded 1977

segments.voice: KLM Flight 4805 was a charter flight from Amsterdam carrying 234 passengers on a holiday excursion to the Canaries. The captain was Jacob van Zanten — a senior KLM instructor pilot and the airline's most experienced 747 captain. His face appeared in KLM's advertising. He was the kind of pilot whose judgment was trusted without question. He had one pressing concern: his crew was approaching the limit of their legal duty time. If they were unable to take off within a certain window, they would be required to rest in Tenerife. He needed to leave.
segments.visual: KLM flight 747 captain van Zanten Amsterdam charter Tenerife

segments.voice: Pan American Flight 1736 was also a charter aircraft — 380 passengers from Los Angeles via New York on their way to Las Palmas for a Mediterranean cruise. Pan Am Captain Victor Grubbs and his crew were experienced and professional. Unlike KLM, they had no pressing duty time issue — they were frustrated by the delay but not under the same pressure. While waiting at Los Rodeos, Pan Am's crew expressed to Tenerife ground control that they were happy to remain at the terminal gate rather than clogging the runway with their aircraft — a request that was declined.
segments.visual: Pan Am 747 captain passengers cruise diversion waiting ground

segments.voice: Dense fog moved across Los Rodeos Airport as the afternoon progressed. Visibility on the runway dropped to 300 meters or less in patches. The airport had no ground radar — no system for controllers to see where aircraft were moving on the surface. The controller was directing multiple large aircraft in a fog that rendered all of them invisible from the tower. Communication was primarily by radio, using a single frequency shared between the tower and both aircraft, creating conditions where simultaneous transmissions could partially or fully mask each other.
segments.visual: airport fog visibility 300 meters no ground radar control tower

segments.voice: Both 747s were taxiing on the runway — the only available route to the runway threshold — in opposite directions. KLM was at the far end, having back-taxied to position. Pan Am was still taxiing along the runway toward the exit point that would allow them to clear the runway before KLM's takeoff. The fog was so thick that neither crew could see the other. The controller could not see either. The position of the Pan Am aircraft relative to the runway exit was uncertain — based on their heading changes and distance traveled rather than confirmed visual or radar position.
segments.visual: runway taxi fog invisible aircraft position uncertain navigation

segments.voice: The sequence of radio exchanges in the final minutes is one of the most analyzed in aviation history. KLM called for takeoff clearance. The controller issued an ATC route clearance — a clearance to follow a specific route after takeoff — but did not issue a takeoff clearance. In aviation phraseology, route clearance and takeoff clearance are different and distinct. Captain van Zanten, however, released the brakes and began the takeoff roll. He understood the route clearance as a takeoff clearance. Or he interpreted it that way. The distinction would kill 583 people.
segments.visual: ATC clearance radio phraseology takeoff route distinction crucial

segments.voice: The KLM flight engineer expressed doubt. He asked van Zanten: 'Is he not clear, then, the Pan Am?' Van Zanten responded: 'Oh, yes' — confirming his belief that the runway was clear. Pan Am's crew heard the exchange and made their own radio call — 'We are still taxiing down the runway' — which should have stopped the KLM takeoff immediately. But at that moment, a transmission from the Tenerife tower — acknowledging a different part of the exchange — partially overlapped the Pan Am transmission on the radio frequency. What van Zanten heard, if he heard anything, was garbled.
segments.visual: radio transmission overlap partial masking simultaneous frequency

segments.voice: Pan Am's crew, emerging from the thickest patch of fog at approximately 1,800 meters down the runway, suddenly saw the landing lights of the KLM 747 in the distance — accelerating toward them. The KLM aircraft was at full power, takeoff roll well advanced. Captain Grubbs turned left — attempting to steer off the runway onto a taxiway — and told his crew to get out. KLM's van Zanten, seeing the Pan Am aircraft suddenly materialize from the fog, pulled back — rotating early, attempting to fly over it. The KLM aircraft's gear cleared the top of the Pan Am fuselage. The underside of the fuselage did not.
segments.visual: 747 lights fog collision takeoff roll impact rotation desperate

segments.voice: The KLM aircraft's engines and lower fuselage tore through the upper section of the Pan Am 747, shearing off the top of the fuselage from above the first class section through to the wing. The KLM aircraft, its structural integrity destroyed and its fuel ignited, climbed momentarily before crashing and burning approximately 150 meters further down the runway. It broke apart and was consumed by fire within seconds. There were no survivors from the KLM aircraft. The Pan Am aircraft burned in place; 61 passengers escaped through holes torn in the fuselage and by jumping from the surviving wing structure.
segments.visual: collision impact both 747s destruction fire runway burning

segments.voice: The investigation — conducted jointly by Spain, the Netherlands, and the United States — took over a year. The Spanish investigation concluded that KLM was responsible — that van Zanten had begun his takeoff roll without clearance. The Dutch investigation emphasized systemic factors, including the quality of radio communications, the phraseology ambiguities, and the absence of ground radar. Both investigations agreed on the essential facts of the collision sequence. The degree of emphasis on individual versus systemic causation differed significantly.
segments.visual: investigation Spanish Dutch American joint report findings

segments.voice: The Tenerife disaster drove the most significant changes in aviation communication standards in the industry's history. The phrase 'take off' was restricted for use only in actual clearances — controllers and pilots were required to use 'departure' in other contexts to eliminate the ambiguity between 'cleared for takeoff' and other messages containing the words 'take off.' Standard phraseology for ATC communications was tightened globally. The International Civil Aviation Organization mandated use of standardized English phraseology by all ATC and pilots on international routes.
segments.visual: aviation phraseology standardization ICAO changes English communication

segments.voice: Crew Resource Management — CRM — training was another direct legacy of Tenerife. The question of why van Zanten's flight engineer's doubt was not acted upon, and why van Zanten himself did not call for an explicit hold when communication was unclear, drove the development of training specifically designed to ensure that crew members at all levels could and would challenge senior pilot decisions. The authority gradient that had perhaps prevented effective challenge on the KLM flight deck became a central focus of the aviation safety training that every airline crew now receives.
segments.visual: crew resource management CRM training aviation cockpit challenge

segments.voice: The Boeing 747 was three years into commercial service when Tenerife happened. Its scale had created scenarios that airports and procedures had not fully adapted to. Los Rodeos was not designed for multiple 747s simultaneously. Its runways, taxiways, and communication systems were adequate for the traffic it normally handled but not for the mass diversion of large aircraft that the Las Palmas bombing produced. The chain of causation runs from a terrorist bomb on another island through an overloaded airport through fog to a radio misunderstanding to 583 deaths.
segments.visual: Boeing 747 scale airport capacity diversion overload chain cause

segments.voice: Captain van Zanten was almost certainly unaware of his own mistake. The CVR shows no hesitation, no second thought — he was a supremely confident, experienced pilot who had processed the communication as a clearance and acted on it. In the moments before the collision, when his flight engineer asked about the Pan Am aircraft, his 'Oh yes' may have reflected genuine belief that the runway was clear — a belief formed on incomplete information, in fog, under time pressure. The most dangerous errors are the ones made by competent people who are certain they are right.
segments.visual: captain confident error certainty competent mistake analysis

segments.voice: The bomb that caused the Las Palmas diversion was planted by the Canarian Independence Movement — a small separatist group that was attempting to draw attention to their cause. The bomb caused minor injuries and significant property damage. The perpetrators had no way of knowing that their bomb in a terminal building would ultimately cause the deaths of 583 people in fog on a different island. The second bomb they had planted at Las Palmas — intended to go off after evacuated passengers returned to the terminal — was found and defused. The causal chain of the deadliest aviation accident in history began with a bomb planted by separatists.
segments.visual: Canarian independence movement bomb Las Palmas causal chain

segments.voice: The sixty-one survivors of the Pan Am aircraft were located principally in the rear sections of the aircraft — the sections not directly impacted by the KLM collision. Several passengers and crew members performed acts of extraordinary bravery in the aftermath, helping injured survivors out of the burning aircraft. The fire that consumed both aircraft burned for hours. The runway was closed. Los Rodeos airport, already overwhelmed before the collision, was now a disaster site.
segments.visual: Pan Am survivors rear section escape fire bravery rescue

segments.voice: The families of 583 victims from dozens of countries — the Netherlands, the United States, Germany, and others — faced a legal and compensation process complicated by the involvement of multiple national jurisdictions, two airlines, and an airport. KLM's insurance covered significant compensation to the victims of both aircraft. The legal processes in multiple countries ran for years. Some families received settlements; some went through prolonged litigation. The question of ultimate financial responsibility — and ultimate accountability — was as fragmented as the investigation's findings.
segments.visual: victim families international legal compensation Netherlands US

segments.voice: Los Rodeos Airport was subsequently expanded and renamed Tenerife North Airport. Its infrastructure was upgraded to handle larger aircraft and more complex operations. Ground radar was installed. The airport is now a major facility. The runway where 583 people died on March 27, 1977 has been used billions of times since. The aircraft parked in the overcrowded taxiways that afternoon, the fog that drifted across the field, the radio frequency crackling with two simultaneous transmissions — they are gone, and memory of them fades with the passage of decades.
segments.visual: Tenerife North Airport modernized expanded ground radar today

segments.voice: The deadliest accident in aviation history — a record that Tenerife has held since 1977 — is a benchmark that the entire industry works to keep from being broken. Every procedural change, every improvement in communication standards, every CRM training program, every ground radar installation can be traced in part to the lessons of Los Rodeos. The accident that aviation has learned from most completely, over the longest period, is still the accident that killed the most people. That record of learning is also a record of what it cost to learn it.
segments.visual: aviation safety record learning Tenerife legacy improvements benchmark

segments.voice: Five hundred and eighty-three people died on a foggy runway in the Canary Islands on a Sunday afternoon. They had set out on holidays, on cruises, on charter flights to the sunshine that the Canary Islands reliably provide. They were diverted from their destination by a bomb they knew nothing about, waited in an overcrowded airport for hours, and died in a collision that resulted from a radio misunderstanding that lasted less than thirty seconds. The lesson is not that flying is dangerous — it is far safer per kilometer than almost any other form of transport. The lesson is that complex systems in stressed conditions, with inadequate information and ambiguous communication, can fail catastrophically even when everyone involved is trying to do the right thing.
segments.visual: memorial Tenerife aviation victims sunset sky reflection peaceful
